title: Stagflation fears rise as OECD lowers Australian forecasts
full_text:
Australia is at risk of stagflation and the longest per-capita recession in more than half a century, according to top economists, following official figures that showed weaker-than-expected 0.3 per cent economic growth in the March quarter and a downgrade from the OECD.
Australia’s inflation rate will hit 5 per cent and economic growth is forecast to deteriorate further this year as the Iran war and higher interest rates cut consumption, the Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development said on Wednesday.
Jim Chalmers said the figures showed that “obviously we can expect some challenging times ahead. We’ve made that clear on a number of occasions.” The agency released downbeat forecasts that showed inflation would hit 5 per cent before ending up at 4.4 per cent over 2026 – higher than its previous forecast of 4.1 per cent and 1.7 percentage points higher than it predicted in December.
GDP growth is projected to slow to 1.9 per cent in 2026, down from the pre-war forecast of 2.3 per cent, in part due to weaker growth in consumption of 1.4 per cent. Next year will end up worse at 1.8 per cent GDP growth, also down from the pre-war forecast of 2.3 per cent.
“Apart from the risk of a more serious disruption of oil and gas production and shipping from the Middle East than assumed in the projections, Australia-specific risks include the possibility that the impact of monetary policy tightening on consumption and other interest-rate-sensitive spending is greater than projected,” the Paris-based organisation said.
“With pre-conflict headline inflation already above target and the Reserve Bank of Australia in a tightening phase, further policy rate increases are likely as inflation is pushed higher by the energy supply shock.” On Wednesday, following the release of the latest national accounts, economists noted Australia was now in the longest per-capita recession and stagflation was a real risk.
Challenger chief economist Jonathan Kearns said: “This is now the longest per-capita recession (a period of lower GDP per capita) in the 65 years of quarterly GDP data, outlasting the significant recessions of the early 1990s (1989-1992) and early 1980s (1981-1984).” Citi economists Faraz Syed and Joshua Williamson said the stagflation risk had grown.
“We anticipate a significant slowdown in consumption due to rising interest rates and cooling housing markets, with dwelling investment likely to contract. The Q1 national accounts belies the impending stagflation risk,” the economists said.
AMP economist Diana Mousina said the March quarter was softer due to lower discretionary spending. “No doubt there will be concerns of a per-capita recession now – we have had plenty of those in the past without an actual technical recession occurring,” Ms Mousina said.
GDP per capita was down 0.1 per cent during the March quarter, leaving the annual figure at 1 per cent.
ANZ economist Adam Boyton said the March figures contained “the seeds” of softer growth over the rest of this year.
“While household consumption rose 0.5 per cent in the quarter, that largely reflected a rise in essential spending as energy rebates came to an end,” Mr Boyton said. “Discretionary consumer spending was weak, rising just 0.1 per cent in the quarter. Real household income growth is also moderating rapidly.” Economists had expected 0.4 per cent economic growth for the quarter and 2.6 per cent for the year, after 0.8 per cent growth in the previous quarter.
Official figures showed that the key measure of productivity, output divided by hours worked, collapsed by 0.6 per cent in the March quarter after recording no growth in the December quarter. The annual growth rate in productivity is now 0.3 per cent, down from 1 per cent in the year to December.
The economic growth figures, which covered a period in which two interest rate hikes were announced, also showed household spending rose 0.5 per cent. The consumption figures reflect a period before the Albanese government’s proposed tax changes were announced.
The Australian Bureau of Statistics noted that income tax had increased 0.7 per cent in the March quarter and was almost 8 per cent higher in the 12 months to March, saying “the increase in income tax and interest payments detracted from growth”.
The Treasurer said on Wednesday that tax cuts would reverse that in coming months. “The total income tax take is up a bit because people are working more and earning more, and because of our tax cuts they will be keeping more of what they earn as well,” the Treasurer said. “This obviously precedes another tax cut which comes in on 1 July and another two tax cuts which are in the legislation before the House.” Dr Chalmers said the biggest takeaway of the national accounts was the growth in the private sector. “Despite all the doomsayers and everyone who wants to talk the Australian economy down, we’re seeing a boom in private investment and that’s a good thing,” he said. “Economic growth was driven by strong growth in business investment, solid consumption and ongoing growth in dwelling investment.” He said investment would “flow through into our economy”, which will be “an important part of shifting what has been a couple of decades now of poor performance on productivity”.
Private investment grew 3.6 per cent in the quarter, contributing 0.7 percentage points to GDP growth. There was 16.3 per cent increase in machinery and equipment investment in the quarter driven by data centre investment.
Government investment also grew, 0.9 per cent in the quarter, contributing 0.1 percentage points to growth. Government spending growth has eased recording -0.2 per cent growth in the quarter.
Both Treasury and the Reserve Bank have warned of a big hit to economic growth from the Iran war. The RBA expects GDP to be just 1.3 per cent by year’s end, while Treasury expects GDP of 1.75 per cent for 2026-27.
